posts.name: Eric Guido
posts.cooked:
Hey everyone, it’s been way too long.

I’m really sorry that I don’t get a chance to hang out here as much as before. I hope that will change when things slow down, but I feel like I’ve been saying that for over a year.

On to why I’m posting. Some of my fellow Barolo lovers put togehter a pretty impressive flight of Bartolo Mascarello that spanned over 50 years. It was one heck of an evening and I though I’d share my thoughts on the vintages with you.

As usual, this is up on my blog and if you want the whole writeup with photos and my own ranting, then please do visit: The Cellar Table

For me, the real standouts were '55, '58, '82, '90, '95, '99, '05, and '06. As for wine of the night, for me it was the '99 with the '06 just a hair behind it.

On to the notes:

1955 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo Canubbi Riserva - The ‘55 Bartolo was unbelievably youthful at first pour, especially with its gorgeous deep color, yet still perfectly mature, displaying a bouquet of dried flowers, dried cherry, and hints of bitter herbs. On the palate, I found soft textures, with vibrant acid and a flash of dried red berries, before pulling back with a hint of decay. It finished medium-long on tart red berries and a hint of smoke. I could sit with this glass all night. (94 points)

1958 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo Canubbi - The ‘58 worried me, with its completely resolved color showing only a slight red hue. On the nose, a display of earth tones, dried flowers, and musk gave way to hints of maderization. On the palate, herb-infused, tart red fruits gave way to elevated acidity that seemed to touch upon all of the senses. It finished long on dried cherry, cedar, leather and a twang of acidity. It was completely mature and on the decline, but still highly enjoyable on this evening. (92 points)

1982 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - The ‘82 showed just how fickle Nebbiolo can be, coming from a bottle that was opened many hours before serving. My first impression was of a closed and hard wine that wouldn’t reveal its treasures, yet over the course of this tasting it blossomed into an elegant beauty. The nose showed hints of pine and parchment up front, yet gained depth in the glass, as dusty dried flowers turned to dark, mineral-laden red fruits. On the palate, I found a deeply focused expression of dark red fruits with still-youthful tannin. It finished long and drying, yet a bolt of acidity enlivened the senses. This is something of a sleeping giant. (95 points)

1990 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - I’ve always found ‘90 to be a difficult vintage to truly understand, and I’m not sure if this Bartolo has added more questions than answers on this night. The wine itself was tremendous, and it didn’t show any of the attributes I associate with ‘90 Barolo. Here I found deep, yet focused red fruits with dried roses, pine, dusty soil, and balsamic tones, in a feminine and lifted display. On the palate, youthful red fruits were aided by zesty acidity, providing a sensation of pure refinement. As it sat in the glass, its textures seemed to soften and expand while never losing its energy or verve. The finish was long and youthful, with tart red berry fruit lingering on and on. (94 points)

1995 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - It’s not often that a ‘95 crosses my path and leaves a such an impression as the ‘95 Bartolo Mascarello did. This displayed a rich, spicy and red floral bouquet with notes of brown spice, savory cherry and sweet herbs. On the palate, I found silky textures with red berries, minerals and inner floral tones, in a perfectly mature expression of nebbiolo. It finished on dried cherries and floral tones. The ‘95 was simply a pleasure to drink. (94 points)

1996 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - The ‘96 Bartolo was as puzzling at this stage as most wines from this ‘Iron Vault’ of a vintage. The nose showed depths of dark red fruit with soaring minerality and hints of menthol. On the palate, I found refined, yet tightly-wound, concentrated red fruit with saturating acidity and firm tannin. It finished structured and lean with a mix of cheek-puckering acidity and palate-coating tannin. I wanted so badly to like this wine more, especially from its amazing bouquet, but the palate still leaves me questioning if ‘96 fruit has the endurance to outlive those intense tannins and acid. (92 points)

1997 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Another standout from a ripe vintage, the ‘97 displayed a rich, deep and intense nose with mineral-infused red fruits and dried flowers. On the palate, I found a remarkably fresh expression for the vintage, with soft textures which soothed the senses while notes of focused red fruits saturated everything they touched. Dried flowers, tart berry and minerals lasted on on the finish, along with a hint of dried orange peel. Well done. (92 points)

1998 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - It was hard to decide if the ‘98 was a damaged wine or just a bad bottle, as the nose was overtly intense with herbal-infused medicinal cherry and spice. On the palate, I found soft textures with dark red fruits, yet little else and seriously lacking energy. It finished on minerals with a hint of oxidation. I decided to score this, because it was still a serviceable wine, just not what you would expect from Bartolo Mascarello. (87 points)

1999 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - What a pleasure it was to enjoy the ‘99 once again. It’s a truly great wine in the making. Here I found a bouquet of mineral-infused, dark red berry fruit with hints of sweet herbs and spice. With time, dusty floral tones came to the fore. On the palate,a focused wave of red berry fruit with acid and mineral-driven tenacity splashed against the senses, leaving inner dried floral tones and hints of fine tannin. It finished structured and classic, with tart red fruits and dried spice. This was a gorgeous showing, and it’s a wine that anyone who loves Bartolo must have in their cellar. (97 points)

2001 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - The nose showed citrus-tinged red berry and pretty floral tones. On the palate, I found soft textures, unexpected so for an ‘01. There was also a lack of depth. This finish was dry with fine tannin and tart, mineral-infused tannin. I’ve heard stories of the ‘01 being a variable bottle, and tonight’s wasn’t nearly as exciting as my last bottle. (90 points)

2005 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - The ‘05 was gorgeous and a great introduction to Maria Teresa’s winemaking style. The nose was very pretty, and I’d go as far as calling it mesmerizing, showing dusty tart cherry, and exotic floral tones with hints of sweet spice. On the palate, I found lean tart berry lifted by brisk acidity and inner floral tones. It finished on focused, intense red fruit and fine tannin structure, built like a dancer so to speak. This is highly enjoyable already, but sure to drink well for a decade or more. (94 points)

2006 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - The ‘06 was an iron vault of a Barolo, but behind its imposing structure, there was so much potential. The nose displayed deep, dark, spicy red fruit, with dried florals, spice cookie and mint. On the palate, dark, mineral-infused red fruits and rich spices saturated the senses, yet stayed fresh through brisk acidity and refined tannin. It finished long on palate-coating tannin, dried cherry and balsamic tones. This was just a baby, but with 30-40 years of potential. (96 points)

2007 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - As expected, the ‘07 Bartolo Mascarello showed the heat of the vintage, yet managed to do it with grace. The nose was intense with rich depths of red berry fruits, spice cake and sweet florals. On the palate, I found silky-soft textures giving way to ripe cherry with plenty of flesh, sweet spices, hard red candies and stunning acidity which provided energy. The long finish balanced spicy red fruit with hints of sweet tannin, yet remained fresh throughout. (92 points)

2009 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - The nose showed mineral-infused dark red fruits and balsamic tones. On the palate, I found rich black cherry with slick, almost sappy textures in something of a monolithic display. The finish was long with grippy tannin, dark minerality and dried red berries. Unfortunately, the ‘09 does suffer from the heat of the vintage, yet never becomes overwhelming. (91 points)

Posted at: The Cellar Table

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Eric Guido:

1998 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - It was hard to decide if the ‘98 was a damaged wine or just a bad bottle, as the nose was overtly intense with herbal-infused medicinal cherry and spice. On the palate, I found soft textures with dark red fruits, yet little else and seriously lacking energy. It finished on minerals with a hint of oxidation. I decided to score this, because it was still a serviceable wine, just not what you would expect from Bartolo Mascarello. (87 points)


Posted at: The Cellar Table

FWIW, when I visited them several weeks ago, they pointed me to the 1998 as their best reasonably attainable vintage for current drinking.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked: As another data point, one of our group recently opened a bottle of the 98 which showed very poorly; I was convinced that the bottle was heat damaged, but it showed many of the same characteristics you mentioned.

posts.name: Eric Guido
posts.cooked:
DavidZ:
Eric Guido:

1998 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - It was hard to decide if the ‘98 was a damaged wine or just a bad bottle, as the nose was overtly intense with herbal-infused medicinal cherry and spice. On the palate, I found soft textures with dark red fruits, yet little else and seriously lacking energy. It finished on minerals with a hint of oxidation. I decided to score this, because it was still a serviceable wine, just not what you would expect from Bartolo Mascarello. (87 points)


Posted at: The Cellar Table

FWIW, when I visited them several weeks ago, they pointed me to the 1998 as their best reasonably attainable vintage for current drinking.

Antonio Galloni mentioned on his website that he’s seen a large amount of bottle varitation in both the '98 and '01 vintages. I would tend to agree with '01. But this was the first I’ve heard about '98 having problems.

posts.name: Mark Ye
posts.cooked: Who’s got cases of '55 I can buy some off if ?

Antonio Galloni mentioned on his website that he’s seen a large amount of bottle varitation in both the '98 and '01 vintages. I would tend to agree with '01. But this was the first I’ve heard about '98 having problems.

Interesting. These were being imported by Chadderdon back then, no?

posts.name: Eric Guido
posts.cooked:
DavidZ:

Interesting. These were being imported by Chadderdon back then, no?

I believe so, but Antonio’s comment seemed more aimed at the way the 750ml’s were bottles. Stating that '89, '98, '00 and '01 were all irregular and much better from Magnum.

“1989 is another vintage that is almost always better from mag. I don’t think it is the size of the bottle alone, but also something during the bottling process that affected those wines.” Antonio Galloni

link_counts.title: Antonio Galloni presents Vinous | Explore All Things Wine

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Interesting that your '58 seemed so evolved. We had a very youthful-looking bottle a few weeks ago which had a preternaturally dark hue.

posts.name: Peter Hirsch
posts.cooked: Fantastic post, thanks very much!!!

William Kelley:

Interesting that your '58 seemed so evolved. We had a very youthful-looking bottle a few weeks ago which had a preternaturally dark hue.

You never know right. No great wines, just great bottles. What I can say is that the provenance was very good. This bottle was hand delivered by Jamie of Chambers Street.

posts.name: Kelly Walker
posts.cooked:
Eric,

Great notes as usual. Very useful to me as I back-fill older vintages when they become available.

posts.name: Eric Guido
posts.cooked:
Kelly Walker:

Eric,

Great notes as usual. Very useful to me as I back-fill older vintages when they become available.

Now’s the time. God knows how high these wines will go over the coming years. At the moment, with current piedmont inflation, I think we are just seeing the tip of the iceberg.

posts.name: Jeff Cassetta
posts.cooked: Wow, nice!

posts.name: Julian Skeels
posts.cooked:
I’ve been rubbish at posting here, but came across this great thread on what used to be one of my favorite producers while planning my next trip to Piedmont this August. Will be in Barolo and Barbaresco 17-21 August… anyone know of good producers that will be open for visits then???

For me the 58-64 Bartolo period was the best, so thought I’d share some of my more recent notes (plus subsequent vintages for context). Somehow I haven’t made a note on the 64, but will correct this when I next pull it from my cellar

  • 1958 Cantina Mascarello Barolo Riserva - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (27/08/2016)
    A lovely bright pink-red on pouring, with a touch of acetone on the nose that blew off to quickly become cocktail cherry. Very sweet with waves of red fruit dominating, but some dark fruit and tertiary notes of wet leaves, mushroom and underbush hiding underneath. Huge mouthfeel and stunning mid-palate with little air. It becomes dry and the acetone re-emerges with 30mins of air, but after an hour this fades again and is replaced with smoke and raspberry. The wine improved for a further 4hrs in the glass, and evolved a gorgeous mouthfeel but I slightly preferred the 1961 enjoyed recently (it had more power). This 1958 was from my cellar - with perfect fill and label. The VA was just too distracting though. Still worth 96pts, but clear potential for 97-98pts with perfect (or ideally larger) bottles. (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1958 Cantina Mascarello Barolo Riserva - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    A pristine regular bottle from my cellar. Pop and poured, with peak between 1hr and 2hrs in glass. Some VA on pouring, which became sweet cherry and violets after 1hr. Lots of sweet spice including cinnamon and cloves. Herbs, rich and broad with a long sweet finish. After 4hrs the VA returns before becoming sweet liquorice and there begins a gentle decline. An outstanding bottle, which for me is the best Bartolo ever made. Again better than recent bottles of 64 and 61 from my cellar. This bottle showed no signs of tiredness, a fantastic showing. Good to see my last note (from another bottle in my cellar) was spot on!! 99pts. (99 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1961 Cantina Mascarello Barolo Riserva - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (29/07/2016)
    Opened a bottle from my cellar - perfect levels and cork (original) and decanted for 2-3hrs. On opening it had fantastic power in the month and seemed to need some air (but not lots, not too much tanin). However it seemed to loose the power after 2hrs. We finished the bottle in 30 mins and it didn’t really shine to be honest. Perhaps it needed longer? Very good, but not excellent on this showing. Next time drink at home and take notes from opening for 5hrs. Am sure there is more upside here… (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1970 Cantina Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From 1.9L bottle. Noticeably rich in the mouth with herbs and mint. The sweetest of all Bartolo wines I have tried. With air this shows a touch of hazelnut and smoke. As accessible as the 1974 Bartolo is, I think this has a bit more potential here (albeit less classic than the 1978). Drink from 2025. 94-95pts. (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1971 Cantina Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From 1.9L bottle. A very disappointing vintage for Bartolo. Showing some VA, herbs and sweet balsamic - but this seems much more extracted than usual for Bartolo, and medicinal notes show with air. Lacks minty freshness on the finish too. Tannins are a little tough and become drying with air. Whilst the 74 Bartolo over-delivers, this was a surprisingly poor showing for a 71. Drink now. 90pts. (90 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1974 Cantina Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From 1.9L bottle. Very elegant and accessible - a great example of classic mature Barolo (if not of Bartolo’s style in my opinion). Cigar and smoke on the nose, rich and sweet balsamic in the mouth with lots of tertiary wet leaves. Long finish, and no VA. Whilst the 71 was the most disappointing Bartolo in their inconsistent 70s, this 74 was the greatest positive surprise. Showing very well now, albeit with less potential than the 78 and 70 Bartolos. I would this should be drunk up from regular bottle formats. Drink now to 2025. 94pts (94 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1978 Cantina Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From magnum. The most floral mature vintage of Bartolo that I can recall, with violets and a touch of dust on opening. Becomes saline and crushed sea-shell in the mouth. With more air it becomes sweeter (sweet balsamic) with tar developing. Doesn’t drink as well now as 1974 Bartolo but I feel has great potential (and more than the 1970). Drink from 2025. 95+pts. (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1982 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From magnum. Esters (banana) and VA (sweaty socks) on the nose initially, which goes away with air. Noticably lighter than similar Bartolo vintages, with less concentration than expected and a slightly watery back-palate. It does become a touch richer with air, with wet leaves, truffle and sweaty leather providing interest - but I am not sure this will improve with more time in the cellar. Together with the 88, this was one of the more disappointing Bartolo vintages for me. Drink now-2030. 93pts. (93 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1985 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From magnum. Very deep colour and the most Monforte-like Bartolo in character that I can recall. Not at all feminine. Dense, rich and deep with breadth, mint, tar and herbs in the mouth. Nose is initially very weak (faint pecorino) and needs another decade or more to develop. Shuts down hard, opening after another few hours of air. Peak not until 2035 here. 96+pts. (96 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1988 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From magnum. More herbs on the nose than most Bartolo vintages. Savoury with noticeably smooth tannins. Plenty of iodine too, with sweet balsamic and eventually tar showing with air - when the wine also shows more tertiary notes (wet leaves) and a very slight touch of the Bartolo mintiness. An approachable vintage, but lacking some complexity and age-worthiness for me. I expected this to have shown better. Drink now-2030. 93-94pts. (94 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1989 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From magnum. Initial nose of Bovril, very meaty, savoury but still with Bartolo’s distinct minty freshness. Becomes tighter with air, showing some VA, before some smoke emerges. Needs more time in cellar but should become spectacular in another 10-20yrs. Over time, this was increasingly superior to the great 1985 (the other good 80s Bartolo). From from 2030. 96-98pts (97 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 1997 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From magnum. Noticeably more tannic and astringent than the 2000 Bartolo. Chewy, but with minty freshness. Needs time to open and has some upside but doesn’t show particularly great potential. Peak from 2030. 91pts? (91 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2000 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (30/11/2016)
    From magnum. Very smooth with little air, showing saline, blood and iron. More tar, herbs and balsamic with air but it also then becomes a little dilute (and fades with extended air). This might be one of the least age worthy Bartolo’s I can recall. Reminds me most of their 88 but with a bit less concentration and lacks the minty freshness on the finish. I would drink this over the next decade. Drink now-2025. 92-93pts. (92 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2011 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (21/03/2016)
    Very open and, whilst it will last 20yrs, I do not think this will improve materially after 2020 (and may not shut down). Light red fruit on the nose. Sweet tannins, lower acidity than usual. Ripe and broad with a great mouthfeel. Lots of cranberry in the mouth, but finish not yet persistent and lacking complexity. Should come though - glass smelt fabulous after wine was finished (with added raspberry, rose and mint).

The 2012 Barolo is shaping up very well, but looks to need longer in the cellar than this 2011. The 2013 Barolo could be a cracker - ripe like 2010 but more voluptuous. (94 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2011 Bartolo Mascarello Barolo - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barolo (23/09/2016)
    My last tasting note detail was spot on - had another bottle at home. This drinks already although will improve to 2020. Very smooth and seductive with this vintage marked by a mint-characteristic in the finish to make it refreshing. Drink now-2030. 94pts (94 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
I was not clearly satistfied with the Bartolo Mascarello Cannubi Riserva 1957 drunk in 2007 (a weak wine).

Same disapointment for the 2006 drunk in november 2015, unbalancedand really seeming too old for its age (a weak bottling ?). I will test another bottle (I bought several bottles at the domain).

My favorite young wines :
2005 (18,5/20 in may 2016)
2011 (17/20 - november 2015, at the domain).

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
Julian, I’m curious about your use of past tense saying Bartolo “used” to be one of your favorite producers. Is it no longer?

Laurent, I own but have not tried the 2006. From everything that I have read, unlike many other recent vintages, it’s built for the long haul. Perhaps you caught it in an awkward period.

posts.name: Julian Skeels
posts.cooked:
Michael S. Monie:

Julian, I’m curious about your use of past tense saying Bartolo “used” to be one of your favorite producers. Is it no longer?

Laurent, I own but have not tried the 2006. From everything that I have read, unlike many other recent vintages, it’s built for the long haul. Perhaps you caught it in an awkward period.

After buying lots of the wines for ages (vertical back to the 50s, etc) they suddenly stopped my allocation, and I was instead offered to buy them in the secondary market for about 3x the price. I sold some of my spare back vintage cases to do this for a year or two and then just gave up on them. I have more in my cellar of great years like 58 than even the cantina has, so I don’t need to beg or go out of pocket to keep buying their young wines that are becoming increasingly expensive - and there are plenty of other Barolo producers I like just as much anyway. Shame but hey-ho.

fancy_title: Exploring Three Generations of Bartolo Mascarello (Long / Boring… Unless you love Barolo)
title: Exploring Three Generations of Bartolo Mascarello (Long / Boring... Unless you love Barolo)
